{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56527","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56527/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56527/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56527/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56527","id":2649540875,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6d7MkL","number":56527,"title":"Undesirable automatic conversion from`Vector{Vector{T}}` to `Vector{T}`","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-11-11T14:40:01Z","updated_at":"2024-11-11T17:12:52Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I accidentally stumbled on the following:
```jl
julia> v = Union{Vector{Int}, Nothing}[Vector{Int}[] for _ in 1:3]
```
This returns a `Vector{Union{Vector{Int}, Nothing}}` but, really, I think it should error. The issue is that the elements in `[Vector{Int}[] for _ in 1:3]` are _not_ `Vector{Int}` but `Vector{Vector{Int}}`, so we have scenario of the kind `Union{A, B}[c::C for c in ...]` with `C` not a subtype of `A` or `B`.

The reason this works is that each of the `Vector{Int}[]` elements are passed on to the conversion machinery. In particular, as pointed out by @thofma on Slack, this is probably passed to one of these methods:

```jl
julia> convert(Vector{Int}, Vector{Int}[]) # Int64[]
julia> Vector{Int}(Vector{Int}[]) # Int64[]
```

It's hard to see where this would be desirable though. Additionally, the conversion only works if the to-be-converted-array is empty. That is, the following fails:
```jl
julia> convert(Vector{Int}, Vector{Int}[[1,2,3],[4,5]])
julia> Vector{Int}(Vector{Int}[[1,2,3],[4,5]])
```
It seems strange and error-prone to have conversion methods that only work for empty input.

functionality"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":11,"created_at":"2024-02-06T20:35:13Z","updated_at":"2024-02-20T14:51:14Z","closed_at":"2024-02-06T22:03:04Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"By default, it appears that work is assigned to the interactive thread pool (if it exists) rather than the default thread pool.  The expected behavior is that, by default, work gets assigned to the default thread pool, and assignment to the interactive thread pool requires either appropriate use of the interactive REPL, or use of `Threads.@spawn :interactive`.  Please also see a corresponding [discourse post](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/understanding-use-of-interactive-thread-pool/109815).

Here are some examples that reproduce the problem:

## Example 1
```bash
julia -t 1,1 -e 'print(stdout, "$(Threads.threadpool())\n")'
```
outputs:
```
interactive
```

## Example 3
```bash
julia -t 1,1 -e 'Threads.threadid() in Threads.threadpooltids(:interactive) ? println("interactive") : println("default"); println(isinteractive())'
```
outputs
```
interactive
false
```

## Example 4
```
julia -t 3,1 -E 'Threads.threadpool()'
:interactive
```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53417","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53417/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53417/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53417/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53417","id":2146673633,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5_86Ph","number":53417,"title":"mapreduce with multiple arrays allocates","user":{"login":"roflmaostc","id":19436526,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5NDM2NTI2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19436526?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/roflmaostc","html_url":"https://github.com/roflmaostc","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/roflmaostc/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/roflmaostc/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/roflmaostc/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/roflmaostc/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/roflmaostc/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/roflmaostc/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/roflmaostc/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/roflmaostc/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/roflmaostc/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-02-21T12:45:08Z","updated_at":"2024-02-21T16:25:11Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi,

With Julia 1.10.1 I noticed that `mapreduce` with two arrays allocates:
```julia
julia> x = randn((512,512));

julia> y = randn((512, 512));

julia> g(x) = x^2
g (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @time mapreduce(g,+,x)
  0.031165 seconds (16.85 k allocations: 1.149 MiB, 99.57% compilation time: 100% of which was recompilation)
261797.64934712922

# no signficant allocation, perfect
julia> @time mapreduce(g,+,x)
  0.000168 seconds (1 allocation: 16 bytes)
261797.64934712922

julia> f(x,y) = x * y
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> mapreduce(f, +, x, y);

# bad allocations
julia> @time mapreduce(f, +, x, y);
  0.001142 seconds (6 allocations: 2.000 MiB)
```

Looking at the [implementation](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/4e7294423f4462244617af6f9eea425bd06a78a6/base/reducedim.jl#L329) there is no specialization. 
Maybe we should adapt the over promising docstring then?
```
mapreduce is functionally equivalent to calling reduce(op, map(f, itr); init=init), but will in general execute faster since no intermediate collection
  needs to be created. See documentation for reduce and map.
```

Related to #38558 ?

Best,

Felix","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53417/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53417/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1957191729","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53417#issuecomment-1957191729","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53417","id":1957191729,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM50qGAx","user":{"login":"raminammour","id":20540368,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIwNTQwMzY4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/20540368?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/raminammour","html_url":"https://github.com/raminammour","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/raminammour/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/raminammour/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/raminammour/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/raminammour/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/raminammour/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/raminammour/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/raminammour/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/raminammour/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/raminammour/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-02-21T16:25:11Z","updated_at":"2024-02-21T16:25:11Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Not sure if it covers all the use cases but maybe change this https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/4e7294423f4462244617af6f9eea425bd06a78a6/base/reducedim.jl#L329-L330
to
```
mapreduce(f, op, A::AbstractArrayOrBroadcasted...; kw...) =
    reduce(op, Iterators.map(f, A...); kw...)
```
The lazy map won't allocate, as promised in the documentation. The manual version still seems faster though. computation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-09-26T06:56:08Z","updated_at":"2023-09-28T12:01:48Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"It is great that `@threads` now supports (and uses by default) `:dynamic` scheduling. As a mirror to that, it would make quite a bit of sense if `@distributed` could handle dynamic scheduling. As I currently understand it, the best way to currently achieve this would be:

```julia
using Distributed

addprocs(whatever)

@everywhere function dynamicforeach(f, channel)
    while isopen(channel)
        try
            task = take!(channel)
            if isnothing(task)
                close(channel)
            else
                f(task)
            end
        catch e
            e isa InvalidStateException && e.state === :closed && break
            rethrow()
        end
    end
end

tasks = 1:20

channel = RemoteChannel(() -> Channel{Union{eltype(tasks), Nothing}}(length(tasks)+1))
foreach(t -> put!(channel, t), tasks)
put!(channel, nothing) # Sentinel, end-task

@everywhere dynamicforeach(task -> println("did $task"), $channel)
```

It would be both much nicer, and I think rather appropriate if `@distributed` accepted a `:static`/`:dynamic` scheduling argument like `@threads` does, and allowed for the following instead of the above:

```julia
@distributed :dynamic for task in 1:20
    println("did $task")
end
```

Going further, I think that `:dynamic` could actually be a sensible default for distributed scheduling.

Xref: #17887 for having a consistent distributed/threaded API
Xref: #41966 for tedious channel taking
Xref: #48515 for iterating a `RemoteChannel`
Xref: #33892 for maybe using a `CachingPool` In NeuralPDE you normally define the PDE ( `rhs = lhs`) symbolically and the loss function is built internally, loosely  `\int dvars ||lhs(vars) - rhs(vars)||^2`.

Is there a way to specify the objective function symbolically directly? Let’s say you are crazy enough and just want to optimize 
`\int dvars cos(lhs(vars) + sin(rhs(vars))` . What would be the easiest way to do that?

@ChrisRackauckas directed me to open an issue to maybe add some support/API to that without having to hack too much code.
